source_info.section: **Risk and Prognostic Factors in Very Old Patients with Sepsis Secondary to Community-Acquired Pneumonia**

population: Patients hospitalized with CAP in a Spanish multicenter cohort
notes: 38% of hospitalized CAP patients presented with severe sepsis. Other independent risk factors included COPD, renal disease, and alcohol abuse. Prior antibiotic therapy and diabetes mellitus were protective.

population: Patients hospitalized with CAP in a Spanish multicenter cohort
notes: Part of a Spanish multicenter cohort study; 38% of patients presented with severe sepsis.

population: Patients hospitalized with CAP in a Spanish multicenter cohort
notes: Part of a Spanish multicenter cohort study; 38% of patients presented with severe sepsis.

population: Patients hospitalized with CAP in a Spanish multicenter cohort
notes: Part of a Spanish multicenter cohort study; 38% of patients presented with severe sepsis.

population: Sepsis patients (multicenter study, age subgroup analysis)
notes: Prompt therapy within the first six hours of resuscitation was associated with a mortality decrease in very elderly (≥80 years) patients with sepsis.

population: Not reported
outcome: 30-day mortality (primary); in-hospital mortality, 1-year mortality, ICU admission, ICU mortality, need for mechanical ventilation (secondary)
notes: Primary outcome: 30-day mortality. Secondary outcomes: in-hospital mortality, 1-year mortality, ICU admission, ICU mortality, and need for mechanical ventilation.

population: Patients with community-acquired pneumonia (CAP)
predictor: Gender, smoking status, alcohol consumption, influenza vaccine, pneumococcal vaccine, previous inhaled corticosteroids, previous systemic corticosteroids, previous antibiotic within a week of admission, chronic pulmonary disease, chronic cardiovascular disease, chronic renal disease, chronic liver disease, chronic neurological disease, diabetes mellitus, nursing home residency
method: Univariate and multivariable logistic regression with backward stepwise selection; Hosmer-Lemeshow goodness-of-fit test
notes: 15 predetermined variables used in propensity score calculation for inverse probability of treatment weighting. p-in < 0.05, p-out > 0.10 for backward stepwise selection.

population: Patients with sepsis and community-acquired pneumonia (CAP)
predictor: Risk factors identified from sepsis model including gender, smoking status, alcohol consumption, influenza vaccine, pneumococcal vaccine, previous inhaled corticosteroids, previous systemic corticosteroids, previous antibiotic within a week of admission, chronic pulmonary disease, chronic cardiovascular disease, chronic renal disease, chronic liver disease, chronic neurological disease, diabetes mellitus, nursing home residency
method: Generalized linear model with binomial distribution and logit link function; inverse probability of treatment weighting; multivariable weighted logistic regression
notes: Propensity score developed to account for observed confounders. Year of admission incorporated into final multivariable model. Text excerpt is incomplete.

population: Patients admitted with a condition where 30-day mortality was assessed
predictor: Risk factors identified via univariate analyses (p < 0.10) with stepwise backward elimination
method: Multivariable (weighted) logistic regression with stepwise backward elimination using Akaike's Information Criterion
notes: Weighting and year of admission were incorporated into the model. Non-significant variables were dropped until no further improvement of AIC was achieved.

population: Very old patients (≥80 years) with community-acquired pneumonia (CAP)
outcome: Sepsis prevalence in very old CAP patients
notes: Table 1 summarizes demographic and clinical characteristics by presence or absence of sepsis

population: Patients with severe CAP (sepsis group), excluding those with septic shock, DNR orders, or missing DNR data
performance: AUC 0.71 (95% CI 0.65–0.78) for the multivariable model
notes: Diabetes mellitus was independently associated with DECREASED 30-day mortality. 61 patients with septic shock excluded; 207 patients with DNR orders excluded; 68 with missing DNR data excluded. All variables remained significant after bootstrapping with small 95% CIs around original coefficients.

population: Very old patients with community-acquired pneumonia (CAP)
effect_size: Protective factor (direction of effect not quantified in excerpt)
notes: Prior antibiotic therapy was protective against sepsis in very old patients with CAP. Unmeasured confounders acknowledged (e.g., patients with better family care treated earlier). Dementia and other comorbidities may mask clinical presentation, delaying antimicrobial administration.

population: Very old patients with community-acquired pneumonia (CAP) with and without sepsis
effect_size: Higher in-hospital mortality in sepsis group

population: Very old patients with community-acquired pneumonia (CAP) with and without sepsis
notes: No significant difference in 30-day and ICU mortality rates between sepsis and non-sepsis groups. Mortality rate due to sepsis in the elderly reported to be 1.3–1.5 times higher than in younger patients.

population: Patients with CAP-related sepsis

study: Referenced study of critically ill patients
population: Critically ill patients
notes: Study suggested diabetes may have a protective effect in critically ill patients, possibly related to higher tolerance of sustained moderate hyperglycemia and greater adaptability to glycemic fluctuations.
